text: Soumya Das is an AI Engineer. AI Engineer focused on LLM infrastructure, distributed systems, and edge deployment. Currently at SuperAlign.ai, building benchmarking pipelines across 100+ GPUs.
Soumya Das's professional tagline is "Building intelligent systems that scale". Soumya Das is based in India.

Soumya Das works as AI Engineer Intern at SuperAlign.ai (Nov 2025 - Present). Key contributions: Building distributed LLM benchmarking systems across 100+ GPUs; Managing bare-metal resources with Proxmox and Coolify; Optimizing inference pipelines using vLLM and ONNX runtime.
Soumya Das works as Founder at ByteBeam (Jul 2025 - Present). Key contributions: Prototyping LiFi high-speed optical communication systems; Leading hardware-software integration for IoT applications.
Soumya Das is pursuing a B.Tech in Electronics and Communication Engineering from Motilal Nehru National Institute of Technology Allahabad (NIT Allahabad) (2022 - 2026) with a CGPA of 8.64.

Soumya Das built "PII & PHI Detection Browser Agent": Browser extension using transformer models for real-time detection of sensitive data. Optimized for edge deployment with ONNX runtime. Technologies used: DeBERTa-v3, ONNX, PyTorch.

Soumya Das built "Noties v2.0": Local AI meeting assistant with real-time transcription using Whisper. Captures audio via WASAPI loopback for privacy-first note-taking. Technologies used: Whisper, WASAPI.
Soumya Das built "Queuebit": Asynchronous document processing pipeline with job queuing. Built on Redis streams and BullMQ for scalable batch operations. Technologies used: Redis, BullMQ.
Soumya Das's Languages skills include: Python, C++, Kotlin, TypeScript, SQL.
Soumya Das's Frameworks skills include: PyTorch, React, FastAPI, Node.js, LangChain.
Soumya Das's ML/AI skills include: Transformers, ONNX, vLLM, Whisper, DeBERTa.
Soumya Das's Infrastructure skills include: Docker, Redis, AWS, Linux, Proxmox, Coolify, LLMOps.
Soumya Das's Tools skills include: Git, VS Code, Linux CLI.

From Soumya Das's blog post "distributed llm benchmarks", section "The Problem": Running LLM evaluations on a single GPU is fine for small models, but when you're benchmarking 70B+ parameter models across multiple tasks, you need distributed infrastructure.

From Soumya Das's blog post "distributed llm benchmarks", section "Architecture": We built a pipeline that: - Distributes evaluation tasks across 100+ GPUs using a custom job scheduler - Uses vLLM for high-throughput inference - Aggregates results in real-time via Redis streams python  Simplified job distribution for gpu_node in cluster.nodes:     job = assign_benchmark_task(gpu_node, model, dataset)     gpu_node.submit(job)

From Soumya Das's blog post "distributed llm benchmarks", section "Key Lessons": 1. Network is the bottleneck - GPU compute is fast, but moving data between nodes is slow 2. Batch sizing matters more than you think - Finding the right batch size per GPU model is critical 3. Monitor everything - We built a Grafana dashboard that tracks per-GPU utilization in real-time

From Soumya Das's blog post "distributed llm benchmarks", section "Results":  Model  Single GPU  Distributed (100 GPUs)  Speedup  -------------------------------------------------  LLaMA-70B  12 hrs  8 min  90x   Mistral-7B  45 min  30 sec  90x  The speedup isn't perfectly linear due to communication overhead, but it's close enough for practical use.
From Soumya Das's blog post "distributed llm benchmarks", section "What's Next": We're working on auto-scaling the cluster based on evaluation queue depth. More on that in a future post.

From Soumya Das's blog post "edge deployment onnx", section "Why Edge Deployment?": Running NLP models in the browser eliminates server costs and latency. With ONNX Runtime Web, you can run transformer models directly in the user's browser.

From Soumya Das's blog post "edge deployment onnx", section "The Pipeline": 1. Train model in PyTorch 2. Export to ONNX format with dynamic quantization 3. Load in browser via onnxruntime-web python  Export to ONNX import torch model = load_model("deberta-v3-base") dummy_input = tokenizer("sample text", return_tensors="pt") torch.onnx.export(model, dummy_input, "model.onnx", opset_version=14)

From Soumya Das's blog post "edge deployment onnx", section "Performance": After quantization, the model runs at ~50ms per inference on modern browsers. That's fast enough for real-time text classification.

From Soumya Das's blog post "edge deployment onnx", section "Browser Compatibility": - Chrome: Full support with WebGPU backend - Firefox: WASM fallback (slower but works) - Safari: WASM only, ~2x slower than Chrome

From Soumya Das's blog post "edge deployment onnx", section "Takeaways": - Quantization is essential - the full-precision model is too large for browsers - WebGPU is the future, but WASM is the safe fallback - Cache the model binary - it's 40-100MB depending on the model

Soumya Das is currently an AI Engineer Intern at SuperAlign.ai and is open to new opportunities in AI Engineering, ML Infrastructure, and LLM systems. Soumya Das has experience with distributed systems, ONNX deployment, and LLM benchmarking.
